notes: Heatmap signal, not instruction to split every large file.
Early backlog framing prepared later domain/meta contract work, but the vocabulary was not yet explicit.
Review debt was translated into bounded FTR work rather than a mechanical cleanup wave.
Formula placement and workbook-meta serialization reduced the immediate layer/meta boundary risks from REVIEW-001.
JSON workbook-meta serialization and writer quick wins reduced concrete spreadsheet-boundary failure modes.
Backend dispatch cleanup reinforced the adapter boundary shape later confirmed by REVIEW-002.
Pass/order, dispatch, registry, and runner ownership were clarified without broad code churn.
Consumer/demo integration work contributed to the later adoption-surface concern, but it was not yet the main steering focus.
Projection and workbook-view slices exercised model-to-workbook editing surfaces under real roundtrip pressure.
ODS parity work turned backend portability from an abstract goal into tested comparison pressure.
Selected current finding for adapter/spreadsheet-boundary watch state.
FK-helper split pressure foreshadows later domain/meta ownership and lifecycle semantics.
Selective package splits with guards became the preferred method for paying down module-size and package-surface debt.
Render-input mutation was a cross-render/write-boundary risk and was closed before the beta release path.
Shows the post-review pattern of closing a named residual risk through a focused FTR and tests.
Documentation topology work prepared clearer entrypoints for later public-surface and adoption readiness.
The separation of active, cold, and structured documentation later informed the project-memory governance model.
v0.1.0b6 is the beta baseline for demo capture and versioned documentation context.
The beta line predates the stronger 0.2.0 XLSX/ODS parity posture and acts as comparison baseline.
Registry, carrier, and meta-layer vocabulary became explicit steering material for later semantic clarification.
Carrier terminology clarified the boundary between canonical model state and spreadsheet/backend carrier observations.
Public docs, Pages, user guide, release notes, and demo launch flow reduced adoption-surface risk for the beta cutline.
The beta publication path provides the comparison baseline before later stronger XLSX/ODS parity work.
Release-process and Pages/CI hardening made the public beta path more repeatable and less fragile.
Early release-friction tracking is historical context for later explicit project-memory and governance data modeling.
FK-helper relation policy, registry lifecycle, and policy-driven primitives made domain/meta contracts operational rather than merely architectural.
Docs/demo realignment made the FK-helper policy shift visible to users before the 0.2.0 release notes.
Refactor pressure was handled through explicit contracts and guards instead of broad module churn.
The semantic model gave the domain/meta concern explicit vocabulary, lifecycle categories, adoption criteria, and a first FK-helper contract matrix.
Later project-memory governance reuses the same idea: coarse semantic steering artifacts rather than raw provenance reconstruction.
Supports watch posture without creating immediate portability work.
Not just cosmetic documentation polish.
Selected adoption-surface signal; not a broad examples cleanup directive.
Review-003 findings were converted into release notes, user guide fixes, public-surface cleanup, and readiness checks.
The public-surface review made workbook/projection expectations visible to careful early adopters, but did not yet drive a projection redesign.
Worldbuilding adoption turned the demo/adoption surface from planned documentation into a real consumer feedback loop.
The consumer workbook exposed concrete projection/editing-surface ergonomics rather than abstract projection wishes.
LibreOffice/ODS behavior in a real consumer file produced backend-specific release pressure.
The persistence-boundary and helper-cleanup fixes narrowed metadata lifecycle ambiguity under real adoption pressure.
README link baking, release preparation, Pages fixups, and CLI interrupt UX made the 0.2.0 public path more credible.
Roundtrip invariant tests and helper cleanup repair made projection/readback behavior safer for consumer workflows.
ODS repeated-cell parsing and parser limits reduced concrete LibreOffice/ODS failure modes before the 0.2.0 cutline.
The release carried explicit FK-helper policy semantics, persistence-boundary positioning, and semantic-model documentation into the public cutline.
The release notes position XLSX as primary backend and ODS/Calc as the second backend proving the spreadsheet contract.
0.2.0 made README, Pages, user guide, release notes, demo path, and known limitations credible for careful early adopters.
0.2.0 includes ODS parser robustness and XLSX/ODS roundtrip posture as release-visible backend evidence.
The presentation metadata work clarified parser/renderer responsibilities for visible cell formatting.
Text orientation and column width preservation improved workbook-visible presentation roundtrip behavior observed through Adoption_001.
ODS column-width and text-orientation support strengthened cross-backend parity immediately after the release cutline.
Current domain/meta semantic pressure.
Projection concern should be revisited with workbook-view contract work.
Projection pressure exists, but not enough to justify a package split by itself.
The project_memory consumer keeps an explicit package release context while developing against the local editable checkout.
First implementation slice; not a historical reconstruction.
Problem-space and contract material were separated so domain/meta semantics could be clarified in smaller, contract-backed slices.
Roundtrip walkthrough and information-flow material moved into the Functional Model, giving projection and reimport questions a better problem-space home.
The successful helper-leakage fix made unclear meta lifecycle boundaries visible as a steering problem, not just a local bug.
The issue appeared in reimport-promotion workflow, showing that projection/readback safety depends on explicit helper cleanup semantics.
Unusual implementation friction despite a small successful fix showed that maintainability pressure was semantic, not only structural.
Post-cleanup reviews shifted the next work frontier from package formation to metadata ownership, lifecycle, and explicit contract decisions.
Shared primitives and package extraction repaid localized structure debt without broad rewrite momentum.
FK-helper lifecycle, carrier, policy, provenance, and v1-retirement semantics were made explicit and reconciled across model, registry, docs, and runtime.
The FK-helper model/doc reconciliation reduced public-surface confusion around old v1 behavior and current v2 helper policy.
The FK-helper cluster stopped blocking further domain cleanup by separating genuine lifecycle complexity from historical sediment and compatibility residue.
Legend-blocks lifecycle classification applied the same semantic-refactor method to a second family with durable intent and run-local resolved state.
The slice clarified the acceptable parser/carrier crossing: table.kind classification is load-bearing, while __legend_blocks residue must not become payload/meta truth.
Legend blocks connect compact workbook codes, visible legend tables, and reimport-safe projection behavior.
Functional-model vocabulary now needs explicit boundaries from Semantic Model contract vocabulary to avoid reintroducing ambiguity.
Clear Functional Model vocabulary helps future readers and agents understand what is built, what is target behavior, and what is only conceptual framing.
The Intent-vs-Resolution persistence-boundary rule (prune resolved facets, keep declarative intent) is a core metadata-lifecycle position that later domain/meta clarification slices inherit.

The regressions were carrier-level XLSX readback failures (workbook-view blob unwrapping, empty carrier markers), showing the boundary between canonical meta and spreadsheet carrier observations needed an explicit guard.
Contamination crossed backends (an ODS-produced sidecar poisoned XLSX workbook-view readback), making cross-backend meta persistence a concrete portability signal rather than an abstract one.

The documentation-blind audit drove the maintainability refactor wave: hiding docs made domain module and package-surface readability problems concrete and made previously-too-risky behavior-preserving slices tractable.
The audit produced domain-only findings and reinforced the domain/meta clarification direction through behavior-preserving package formation in the domain layer.

The tooling arc (review-set rendering, commit-signal extraction, health diagnostics, assessment mapping, maintenance cycle docs) operationalised the governance concern into a repeatable bounded cycle.
The current_context report and health diagnostics reduce the raw-history reconstruction pressure on agents and maintainers, improving orientation quality without requiring a full git-log scan.

The unresolved NaN/unresolved-helper contract is a concrete open boundary in the FK-helper domain/meta contract: no defined canonical shape exists yet for a helper value that fails key lookup after reimport without cleanup.

The explicit xfail sentinel prevents the test suite from appearing fully green while an unresolved contract gap exists. Removing or silencing the xfail without resolving the contract would be a hygiene regression.

The gap surfaces on the no-cleanup reimport path: the projection ergonomics concern includes the question of what representation an unresolved helper value should carry after reimport, and this sentinel holds that question open until a contract decision lands.

The review wave produced the Intent-vs-Resolution persistence rule and the meta-flattening hypothesis — both are foundational evidence for how the project resolved domain and metadata semantic ambiguity.

The model boundary reviews identified structural accommodation (Semantic Model hosting problem-space content due to a narrow Functional Model) as the root cause of the boundary blur. The recut improved maintainability by making the contract-vs-explanatory distinction navigational rather than implicit.

The meta-flattening hypothesis is the direct intellectual predecessor to the feature-local FK-helper contract approach. It reframed _meta from a growing ontology into a carrier, which enabled scoped cleanup and falsification-first development rather than global metadata governance.

The persistence-boundary mechanism sits in the Semantic Model by default because the Technical Model has no paired chapter — an architectural inconsistency relevant to how meta semantics are defined and communicated.

Contract-leaning documentation in a problem-space chapter creates a misleading model surface. Tracked by FTR-CONCEPT-DOCS-CLEANUP-P5; the watch is that it might become a precedent for further misrouting.
Empty Technical Model backbone chapters are the highest-leverage maintainability gap under the three-model hypothesis — mechanism content cannot be properly routed until these chapters exist.

The independent assessment explicitly confirmed that consumer adoption evidence pointed at projection management and column-role classification as the primary lever — supporting the CONC-PROJECTION-ERGONOMICS concern priority.
frame_lifecycle provenance fields are Resolution facets under the Intent-vs-Resolution rule — an open meta-semantic boundary question that may require a future persistence boundary extension.

The meta-carrier-not-ontology direction was the foundational framing that made the FK-helper feature-local contract approach work — historical evidence that feature-local semantics are the correct answer to domain/meta semantic pressure.

The meta complexity risk assessment is the strategic framing behind the domain/meta semantics concern: not just a technical tidiness goal but a trajectory concern about maintainability and long-term operability.
The dead-end risk assessment is the motivating context for why Project Memory governance exists at all — it explains the investment decision.
The genesis and scope correction record defines the current governance posture: PM as reasoning-trail rather than automation substrate, and the explicit deferral of LangGraph-style orchestration.

Provenance anchor for the archived June-2026 documentation-blind domain audit and FK-helper/meta-simplification working context; historical input to the domain/meta clarification thread, not current truth.
The inventory preserves the June-2026 implemented-family baseline needed before later Functional Model promotion or semantic cleanup work.
Many inventoried families are projection/workbook-editing capabilities; PM links to the cold-storage inventory without modeling those families here.
This is an intentionally minimal PM linkage: a discoverability and intent anchor only, not a duplicated inventory or new Project Memory hierarchy.
This is the active architectural decision for the domain/meta semantics concern: Functional Model use cases drive the bridge DSL, not current implementation sediment.

The ADR preserves adapters/parser/renderer knowledge but treats the domain/IO seam as a bridge DSL, so future adapter-boundary work must align with the explicit writer/reader/lifecycle/conflict contract.
Project Memory records the decision as a steering anchor with rationale and source links rather than duplicating the ADR or creating a new hierarchy.
Records an unresolved Functional Model/meta-lifecycle gap: runtime resolution may be writeback-scope evidence, but no durable policy is approved.
Missing projection/writeback-scope semantics directly affect workbook editing surfaces when rows, columns, or source entities change during roundtrip.
Keeps the unresolved policy visible so future cleanup does not delete or promote resolution fields before their role is specified.
User-facing reimport safety depends on clear behavior for deleted or renamed projection rows, columns, and underlying entities.
